query: evidence on axitinib use in pancreatic cancer survival

retrieved_docs.title: Pancreatic Islet Cell Cancer(Archived) -- Medical Oncology
retrieved_docs.content: Everolimus bind with the mammalian target of rapamycin (mTOR) to inhibit it. Sunitinib is a multiple receptor tyrosine kinase inhibitor. Both of these drugs are a second-line treatment for patients with progressive pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors. Both of the drugs have shown a significant increase in the progression-free survival and overall survival of the patients. [30] [31]

retrieved_docs.title: Renal Clear Cell Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Axitinib
retrieved_docs.content: It is a multitargeted receptor tyrosine kinase inhibitor, which inhibits VEGFR 1, 2, 3, PDGFR alpha and beta, and c-kit—administered orally in a dose of 5 mg twice daily, with a bioavailability of 58 percent. Metabolized, primarily in the liver, has a half-life of 2.5 to 6 hours and is excreted in the urine (less than 1 percent). Approved for use in the second-line setting by the FDA and EMA in 2012. ESMO Magnitude of clinical benefit scale ranking of 4. [98] [101] [102] [101]

retrieved_docs.title: Upadacitinib -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the mechanism of action of upadacitinib. Evaluate the potential adverse effects of upadacitinib. Determine the appropriate monitoring for patients receiving upadacitinib. Implement effective collaboration and communication among interprofessional team members to improve outcomes and treatment efficacy for patients who might benefit from upadacitinib therapy. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Erlotinib -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Erlotinib is a first-generation receptor tyrosine kinase inhibitor (TKI) approved by the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for the treatment of certain types of non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) and advanced pancreatic cancer.

retrieved_docs.title: Erlotinib -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Pancreatic cancer: The FDA approved erlotinib as a treatment for pancreatic cancer in 2005. Many human pancreatic cancers exhibit the EGFR mutation, and patients with this mutation often have a poor prognosis. Historically, gemcitabine monotherapy was the standard treatment for advanced pancreatic cancer. However, recent studies have shown promising results with combinations of gemcitabine and other anti-cancer agents, including erlotinib. The combination of erlotinib and gemcitabine has demonstrated superior benefits compared to gemcitabine monotherapy for patients with locally advanced or metastatic pancreatic cancer. Consequently, erlotinib, in combination with gemcitabine, is indicated as the first-line treatment for patients with locally advanced, unresectable, or metastatic pancreatic cancer. Erlotinib has not been studied as a monotherapy for pancreatic cancer. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Glucagonoma -- Treatment / Management -- Tyrosine kinase and mTOR-targeted therapy
retrieved_docs.content: Sunitinib and everolimus are approved for progressive, well-differentiated pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors, including glucagonomas. Sunitinib yields objective responses in approximately 10% of patients, resulting in a median progression-free survival of around 11 months. Everolimus offers similar outcomes by inhibiting growth signaling. These treatments are used after disease progression on somatostatin analogues or when extrahepatic metastases make liver-directed therapies unfeasible. Closely monitoring for adverse effects, such as hypertension, cytopenias, mucositis, hyperglycemia, and noninfectious pneumonitis, is essential. [17] [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Pancreatic Cancer -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: If the pancreatic adenocarcinoma is considered locally advanced, then by definition, it is unresectable. Neoadjuvant treatment with chemotherapy and/or radiation is typically preferred in this situation.

retrieved_docs.title: Pancreatic Cancer -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: If the adenocarcinoma of the pancreas is located in the body or tail of the pancreas, then the surgical procedure of choice is distal pancreatectomy with splenectomy. If there is extensive involvement of the celiac artery and splenic artery, a modified Appleby procedure can be performed if it meets the criteria. [11] Minimally invasive approaches to Whipple and distal pancreatectomy with splenectomy have been investigated with similar outcomes in survival, morbidity, and mortality. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Gastrointestinal Neuroendocrine Tumors -- Medical Oncology -- Targeted Therapies with mTOR Inhibitors
retrieved_docs.content: Everolimus, an mTOR inhibitor, has shown efficacy in progressive, well-differentiated pancreatic, GI, or pulmonary NETs by blocking a key intracellular pathway responsible for cell growth and angiogenesis. The RADIANT-3 trial established its role in improving progression-free survival in patients with pancreatic NETs. The RADIANT-4 trial demonstrated similar benefits in nonfunctional lung or GI-NETs. Common adverse events include stomatitis, hyperglycemia, dyslipidemia, fatigue, and immunosuppression—all of which necessitate vigilant metabolic and hematologic monitoring. [25] [31]

retrieved_docs.title: Pancreatic Islet Cell Cancer(Archived) -- Medical Oncology
retrieved_docs.content: The tumor growth has been effectively controlled to an extent with chemotherapy (temozolomide, capecitabine, streptozotocin, dacarbazine, oxaliplatin, 5-fluorouracil), somatostatin analogs (lanreotide and octreotide) and targeted molecular treatment (sunitinib, everolimus, etc.). [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Pancreatic Cancer -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis for pancreatic adenocarcinoma remains poor despite advances in cancer therapy. The 5-year survival rate is approximately 20%. Prognosis after 1 year of diagnosis is dismal, with 90% of patients dying at 1 year despite surgery. However, palliative surgery can be of benefit.

retrieved_docs.title: Renal Clear Cell Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Recent Update
retrieved_docs.content: In an update published in February this year, the European Society of Medical Oncology has endorsed the use of combination therapy with pembrolizumab and axitinib as the frontline therapy for treatment of naïve advanced disease, based upon the results of the Keynote 426 trials, where this combinatorial therapy was compared with sunitinib, irrespective of the IMDC subgroup and the PD-L1 biomarker status. While the same update also makes a recommendation in favor of the use of ipilimumab and nivolumab in the intermediate and low-risk settings after analysis of the long-term survival results (32 months), VEGF targeted tyrosine kinase inhibitor treatment is recommended after progression on PA/IN combinatorial regimens. Targeted therapy is also indicated when the above-mentioned combinatorial therapy cannot be used or is contraindicated. [121]

retrieved_docs.title: Pancreatic Cancer -- Surgical Oncology
retrieved_docs.content: The neoadjuvant first approach in resectable pancreatic adenocarcinoma is implemented more and more frequently at high-volume centers nationwide and internationally. The rationale behind the neoadjuvant first approach is that the patient is in the best shape to receive chemotherapy and at the best odds of completing chemotherapy for 4 to 6 months. In addition, tissue is thought to be well-oxygenated, having not undergone a large procedure such as the Whipple. Many patients do not complete or even start adjuvant chemotherapy after surgical resection, thereby decreasing their survival. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Pancreatic Islet Cell Cancer(Archived) -- Medical Oncology
retrieved_docs.content: FAS combination had a response rate of 39%, which resulted in increased overall survival and progression-free disease. The chosen population was patients with metastatic or locally advanced pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors. [32] A combination of 5-FU/streptozocin with bevacizumab showed overall survival at 24 months and progression-free survival at 23.7 months to be 88% in patients with metastatic or locally advanced pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors. [32]

retrieved_docs.title: Pancreatic Islet Cell Cancer(Archived) -- Medical Oncology
retrieved_docs.content: The patients with recurrent, advanced, or metastatic pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors can often be poor candidates for surgical resection. This makes medical therapy the next best option for pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors. Medical therapy can be either symptom directed or for controlling the growth of the tumor.

retrieved_docs.content: Tumor cells, uncertain whether benign or malignant, represent the abnormal cellular component of السرطان البنكرياسي (pancreatic cancer). Their ambiguous nature underscores the diagnostic challenge in distinguishing benign from malignant lesions within the pancreas, influencing treatment decisions and overall patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 901
retrieved_docs.content: Carcinoma of head of pancreas is identified as a form of الورم السرطاني, indicating that this pancreatic cancer represents a malignant tumor morphology. Metastatic carcinoma to lymph node of head and neck is also classified under الورم السرطاني, confirming its status as a malignant tumor, and it is further associated with the morphology of السرطان النقيلي, emphasizing that this disease involves metastatic cancer spread to lymph nodes in the head and neck region. Together, these relationships illustrate that both the pancreatic head carcinoma and the metastatic head‑and‑neck lymph‑node carcinoma are subsets of malignant tumors, with the latter specifically exemplifying metastatic cancer, a distinction that is important for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2707
retrieved_docs.content: Bard‑Pic syndrome is linked to cancer of the pancreas, indicating that individuals with this syndrome may be predisposed to developing pancreatic malignancy. Cancer of the pancreas is classified as سرطان نقيلي في رأس البنكرياس, a metastatic cancer located in the head of the pancreas. Recognizing this connection helps clinicians understand the potential oncologic risk associated with Bard‑Pic syndrome and underscores the importance of vigilant pancreatic evaluation in affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 158
retrieved_docs.content: other and unspecified primary as well as to organ‑specific cancers including pancreatic cancer (السرطان البنكرياسي) and bone cancer (سرطانة العظم). In contrast, tumor cells, benign are not disease findings for renal, ovarian, pancreatic, bone, and other listed cancers. Renal malignancies (الورم الأورمي الكلوي, سرطان الخلايا الكلوية) frequently co‑occur with nephrectomy (استئصال الكلية), reflecting standard surgical management. Overall, these relationships illustrate how metastatic processes are organized across anatomical sites, primary tumor origins, cellular pathology, and clinical interventions, supporting accurate diagnosis, coding, and optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1905
retrieved_docs.content: Benign tumor cells are not regarded as a finding of pancreatic cancer (السرطان البنكرياسي), indicating that their presence does not support a diagnosis of this malignancy. In contrast, tumor cells whose nature is uncertain—whether benign or malignant—are considered abnormal cells of pancreatic cancer, reflecting their potential relevance to the disease process. Recognizing the difference between clearly benign cells and cells of indeterminate behavior is essential for accurate pathological assessment and appropriate patient management in the context of pancreatic cancer.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2701
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of the head of the pancreas, primary malignant neoplasm of the nasal bone, and primary malignant neoplasm of the thymus are each examples of primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, which constitute pathological processes. These entities represent malignant tumor growth arising in distinct anatomical sites—the pancreatic head, the nasal bone, and the thymus—characterized by uncontrolled proliferation of cancerous cells. As pathological processes, they require oncologic assessment, appropriate staging, and targeted therapeutic strategies to manage the aggressive nature of the malignancy in each location.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2412
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to lymph nodes of the neck originating from a primary malignant neoplasm of the thyroid, primary malignant neoplasm of the head of the pancreas, and primary malignant neoplasm of the thymus are all instances of primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, which are pathological processes characterized by uncontrolled malignant cell growth. Each condition represents a distinct organ‑specific cancer that arises as a primary tumor (thyroid, pancreatic head, thymus) and, in the case of the thyroid cancer, can spread to regional lymph nodes in the neck. Recognizing these entities as primary malignant neoplastic proliferations highlights their shared nature as serious pathological processes that require comprehensive oncologic assessment, staging, and targeted therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1899
retrieved_docs.content: Bard‑Pic syndrome is linked to pancreatic cancer, indicating that this genetic or clinical syndrome has a mapped association with the development of cancer of the pancreas. Recognizing this connection is important for clinicians, as patients diagnosed with Bard‑Pic syndrome may require heightened surveillance for pancreatic malignancy and tailored management strategies to address the potential increased risk.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2718
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate is recognized as a pathological finding associated with السرطان البنكرياسي (pancreatic cancer). The detection of malignant cellular infiltrate in tissue specimens indicates that cancerous cells have invaded pancreatic tissue, thereby supporting the diagnosis of السرطان البنكرياسي. Identification of this infiltrate through histopathological examination informs clinical staging, guides therapeutic decision‑making, and is essential for optimal patient management in cases of pancreatic cancer.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 871
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of head of pancreas is linked to the morphology of ورم خبيث أولي, indicating that this pancreatic cancer represents a specific form of a primary malignant tumor. In addition, سرطانة توتية is classified as a subtype of ورم خبيث أولي, showing that it also falls under the broader category of primary malignant tumors. Together, these relationships highlight that both the pancreatic head neoplasm and سرطانة توتية are particular manifestations within the overarching concept of ورم خبيث أولي.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 4 -- Community 2918
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant tumor cells represent the abnormal cellular component that defines and drives the disease known as السرطان البنكرياسي (pancreatic cancer), indicating that the presence of these atypical, cancerous cells is the hallmark of this specific malignancy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 1033
retrieved_docs.content: Carcinoma of head of pancreas is identified as a malignant tumor (الورم السرطاني) and is directly mapped to the broader term cancer of pancreas. Cancer of pancreas is classified as a metastatic cancer located in the head of the pancreas (سرطان نقيلي في رأس البنكرياس). Likewise, metastatic carcinoma to lymph node of head and neck is described as a malignant tumor (الورم السرطاني) whose associated morphology is that of metastatic cancer (السرطان النقيلي). These interconnections show that both the primary pancreatic carcinoma in the head and the metastatic carcinoma involving head‑and‑neck lymph nodes represent forms of malignant, often metastatic, neoplasms, underscoring the need to recognize their shared pathology for accurate diagnosis and effective patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 1 -- Community 235
retrieved_docs.content: frequently co‑occurs with renal tumors such as الورم الأورمي الكلوي (oncocytoma) and سرطان الخلايا الكلوية (renal cell carcinoma), linking diagnostic terminology with appropriate oncologic surgery. Collectively, these relationships illustrate how malignant cellular infiltrates, metastatic and secondary lesions—particularly in supraclavicular lymph nodes—serve as critical diagnostic markers for thymic carcinoma, pancreatic cancer, renal and ovarian cancers, and bone malignancies, and how multilingual nomenclature and procedural associations support accurate staging, prognosis, and therapeutic decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 1039
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of the head of the pancreas and primary malignant neoplasm of the thymus are both classified as primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, which are pathological processes. As malignant tumors arising in distinct anatomical sites—the pancreatic head and the thymus—each represents an uncontrolled growth of neoplastic cells that characterizes a serious pathological condition. Recognizing these neoplasms as primary malignant neoplastic proliferations emphasizes their role as aggressive cancerous processes that require prompt diagnostic evaluation and targeted oncologic management.
